collapse.note: A wicket after a wicket, pooled over every delivery with a same-innings predecessor: how likely the next ball is a wicket right after one, over the same balls' marginal rate. Below one means a wicket makes the next ball slightly less likely to be another, not more.

controlling_morph.name: duelweb
controlling_morph.new_buffer: pairing.u16 (duel id per point, delivered as uPairingTex)
controlling_morph.note: Free field to the duel web: every ball flies to its duel's strand midpoint (plus a jitter that grows with the duel's ball count) or sinks into the dim dust. The field reads the pairing from a data texture uPairingTex indexed by point-index in-shader (position.x already holds the point index), so no 15th vertex attribute is added and the field holds at 14. The per-duel strand centre and color come from uDuelTex, texelFetch'd by duel id. Four held scalars over the morph: duelReveal, duelDominance, duelDustDim, strandRecede; all default inert so every prior release renders byte-identically.

dominance_color.note: color = clamp((dom - center_mu) / half_range, -1, 1); +1 batter-red, -1 bowler-blue; the value is empirical-Bayes shrunk runs a ball, so most strands land pale

ch9-collapse.text: Commentary loves a collapse: one wicket brings the next. We tested it the way Chapter 8 tested a wicket bringing a wicket, by asking whether a wicket really makes the next delivery more dangerous. It does not. The aftershock read comes out at about 0.95, below one, which means a wicket makes the very next ball slightly less likely to be another, not more, across the 15,176 balls that followed a wicket. That lines up with Chapter 8's finding that a wicket after a wicket runs about 0.93 and never clears shuffled cricket in the modern game, so the collapse is mostly a story we tell after the fact. The self-exciting aftershock read and the shuffle test are the technical names.

ch9-duel.text: A duel is a batter and a bowler who faced each other at least 30 legal balls across the whole history, and there are 1,691 of them between 277 players (244 men and 33 women). Of the 316,199 balls ever bowled, 79,378 live in one of those duels, about a quarter, and the other 236,821 stay as dust. The busiest players sit dead centre because everyone has a rivalry with them: V Kohli with 76 duels, then S Dhawan 65, RA Jadeja 59, RG Sharma 57; players spread by era so the layout reads left to right as a timeline. V Kohli and RA Jadeja met 160 balls for 179 runs and 3 dismissals across 14 seasons (2009 to 2025); the shrunk read comes out to about 1.17 runs a ball, below the league average, so the strand runs bowler-blue at about -0.54. 232 duels have run eight seasons or longer by a strict recount that counts only seasons the pair actually faced a ball, so it ships 232 rather than the blueprint teaser's 235. The color is each pair's runs a ball against the league average of 1.3322, pulled toward the average until the pair has faced about 51 balls of weight, then clamped to plus or minus one, so a thirty-ball duel does not out-shout a hundred-and-sixty-ball one and most strands land pale. Dropping the thirty-ball threshold adds shorter, noisier duels without changing the web's shape; raising it thins the tail but keeps every hub. The men's and women's leagues share no players, so they are laid out as two separate webs and never mixed; the women's web has 33 players. The empirical-Bayes shrinkage and the at-least-thirty-ball threshold are the technical names.

ch9-heartbeat.text: For each team we take the share of last season's squad that is back this season, then average across the league, so a full re-auction drops the line. Across the seasons the overlap ran 2009 0.421, 2010 0.523, 2011 0.175, 2012 0.474, 2013 0.471, 2014 0.191, 2015 0.484, 2016 0.430, 2017 0.479, 2018 0.179, 2019 0.468, 2020 0.426, 2021 0.504, 2022 0.196, 2023 0.430, 2024 0.419, 2025 0.190, 2026 0.458. The five mega-auction years are the five lowest, 2011 (0.175), 2014 (0.191), 2018 (0.179), 2022 (0.196), 2025 (0.190), averaging 0.186 against 0.461 in every other year, a clean gap with the sixth-lowest year (2024) up at 0.419. Franchises are tracked by their canonical identity, so a team that changed its name is not counted as a new squad. The women's league has its own pulse, 2024 0.476, 2025 0.536, 2026 0.257, with its first big reset in 2026. The season scrub lights each strand only in the years its two players actually faced a ball; the web positions never move during the scrub, so the rivalries genuinely span the reset years rather than being redrawn to look continuous. Squad overlap is the technical name.

ch9-loyalty.text: A one-club player is a player in their fourth season or later who has only ever appeared for one franchise, measured season by season; requiring a fourth season keeps out one-season cameos. The one-club share fell from about 27 in 100 in 2012 to about 12 in 2022, roughly a halving, which ships straight rather than the blueprint teaser's 28 to 15. It has ticked back up lately to about 18 in 2026 as the two newest franchises' four-season players are one-club by definition, an honest wrinkle rather than a reversal of the fall. AJ Finch appeared for 9 franchises (DC, GL, KKR, MI, PWI, PBKS, RR, RCB, SRH), the most of any player. A franchise that changed its name is treated as one club, so a player who stayed through a rename is still a one-club player. Distinct franchises across a career is the technical name.

ch9-payoff.text: Your longest rivalry is your franchise's own duel that spans the most seasons, a duel where the batter or the bowler ever wore your shirt. The reset row counts who left and who arrived at your most recent mega-auction. The loyal player is your longest-serving one-club player. A young franchise has fewer decade-scale rivalries, so its longest may be shorter, which is a fact about its age and never a deficit. The women's league card is the forming-fast beat made personal, never a deficit card.

heartbeat.definition: For each team we ask what share of last season's squad is back this season, the count they kept over the count they used across both years, then average across the league. A full re-auction resets almost everyone, so the line drops. The faint band around the line is the lowest and highest single team each season. Franchises are tracked by their canonical identity, so a team that changed its name is not counted as a new squad.

loyalty.definition: A one-club player is someone in their fourth season or later who has only ever appeared for one franchise, measured season by season. Requiring a fourth season keeps out one-season cameos, so the measure is about players with real careers. A franchise that changed its name is treated as one club, so a player who stayed through a rename is still a one-club player.

mystery_handoff_in: Last chapter, beliefs churned and a whole doctrine could arrive and die inside a broadcast cycle. But underneath the churn some things refuse to move. Rivalries. Who a team keeps. This chapter finds what stays.
mystery_handoff_out: Institutions churn and the human fabric holds. One question is still open: was 2023 a genuinely new era, or just a louder version of the old game? Next chapter, the Era Machine settles it.

payoff.definition: Your longest rivalry is your franchise's own duel that spans the most seasons, a duel where the batter or the bowler ever wore your shirt. The reset row counts who left and who arrived at your most recent mega-auction. The loyal player is your longest-serving one-club player. A young franchise has fewer decade-scale rivalries, so its longest may be shorter, which is a fact about its age and never a deficit. The women's league card is the forming-fast beat made personal, never a deficit card.

title: The Living League

wpl.framing: The women's league has its own web, laid out separately because the men's and women's leagues share no players. At three seasons old its rivalries are taking shape as fast as the men's game did at the same age, and it has its own auction pulse. It is a young league whose fabric is forming fast, already knotting together rivalries that will run for a decade.
